reasoning: The primary credit level at the Federal Reserve tends to spike during times of market stress and gradually run off (mean-revert downwards) during normal times. Given the value is at 7,349 million, it is elevated compared to completely benign periods but much lower than crisis peaks, suggesting a slow run-off is structurally more likely than a sudden increase, driving short-run probabilities slightly below 50%. The empirical base rate for FRED questions (0.422) heavily informs the baseline probability. As the forecast horizon increases (from 1 week to 1 month to 3 months), uncertainty grows due to the higher variance of potential macroeconomic developments, drawing the probabilities progressively closer to 0.50.
